summary.title: [Motion] Tailwind transforms snap instead of transitioning
summary.one_line_description: Explicit transform transitions do not animate Tailwind v4 translate and scale utilities, so button presses and section entrances snap.

bug_1.file_locations: - demo/src/components/elements/button.tsx - constants: `TRANSITION`, `ACTIVE_PRESS`, Lines 23-29 - demo/src/components/sections/faqs-accordion.tsx - function: `FAQsAccordion`, Lines 73-93 - demo/src/components/sections/faqs-two-column-accordion.tsx - function: `FAQsTwoColumnAccordion`, Lines 169-196 - demo/src/components/sections/call-to-action-simple.tsx - function: `CallToActionSimple`, Lines 33-52 - demo/src/components/sections/call-to-action-simple-centered.tsx - function: `CallToActionSimpleCentered`, Lines 27-42

issue_description.description: The broad-transition replacement declares `transition-property: transform` while the affected Tailwind v4 utilities compile to the independent CSS `translate` and `scale` longhands. Changing `active:scale-[0.96]`, `translate-y-*`, or `scale-*` therefore does not change the declared `transform` property and is excluded from the transition. Shared buttons reach the correct pressed scale but snap into and out of it instead of using the required interruptible 150ms feedback. FAQ section wrappers and CTA entrances fade because opacity is listed, but their translation and scale components jump immediately, weakening or removing the intended slide and scale choreography. The same mismatch affects every listed location and directly violates the plan requirement to preserve motion while replacing `transition-all`. Replace the property lists with the actual Tailwind v4 longhands used by each state, or express those state changes through the `transform` property itself.
issue_description.expected_vs_actual: | Aspect | Expected | Actual | |--------|----------|--------| | **Button press** | Scale interpolates to and from 0.96 over 150ms | The independent `scale` property snaps because it is not transitioned | | **FAQ entrance** | Opacity and vertical position settle together | Opacity fades while the independent `translate` property jumps | | **CTA entrance** | Opacity, translation, and scale animate together | Only opacity is covered by the explicit property list | | **Transition discipline** | Explicit lists name every changed CSS property | Lists name `transform`, which the Tailwind utilities do not modify |

summary.title: [Navbar] Duplicate dialog commands can throw
summary.one_line_description: The secondary navbar issues native dialog commands and imperative calls from the same click, which can invoke showModal or close twice.

issue_description.description: The secondary navbar attaches both `command="show-modal"` or `command="close"` attributes and React `onClick` handlers that call `dialog.showModal()` or `dialog.close()` directly. Tailwind Plus installs command handling at the document level, so one user click can execute the declarative command and then the React handler. The open handler has no `dialog.open` guard; if the native command opens the dialog first, the subsequent `showModal()` call targets an already open modal and can throw `InvalidStateError`. Duplicate close requests can also bypass or restart the registered leave lifecycle. This component is not mounted by the current runnable layout, but it remains a reusable demo component and fails as soon as a route or template consumes it. The animation guide explicitly prohibits mixing these two command paths. Retain one dialog-control mechanism and remove the other.
issue_description.expected_vs_actual: | Aspect | Expected | Actual | |--------|----------|--------| | **Open action** | One mechanism opens the dialog once | Native command and React handler can both call `showModal()` | | **Runtime safety** | Repeated or overlapping actions remain exception-free | The second `showModal()` can throw for an already open modal | | **Exit lifecycle** | Tailwind Plus owns one reversible leave transition | Duplicate close paths can interfere with lifecycle coordination |

summary.title: [Navbar] Secondary controls remain undersized
summary.one_line_description: The secondary navbar still renders 28-40px navigation targets and 36px menu controls instead of the required 40px desktop and 44px mobile boxes.

issue_description.description: The active centered-logo navbar received explicit minimum target sizes, but the secondary left-aligned navbar retained its original geometry. Desktop links use `lg:text-sm/7` without a 40px minimum, so their target height follows the 28px line box. Mobile links use a 40px line height without the required 44px minimum. The menu and close buttons wrap a 24px icon with 6px padding on each side, producing a 36px square. `NavbarLogo` also has no minimum target box. Although this component is currently dormant in `demo/src/app/layout.tsx`, its exported interface is ready for reuse and would immediately violate the plan's control-size contract on any page that adopts it. Apply the shared 44px mobile and 40px large-screen target geometry to its links, logo, menu button, and close button without overlapping adjacent targets.
issue_description.code_example: ```typescript // File: demo/src/components/sections/navbar-with-logo-actions-and-left-aligned-links.tsx // Lines 91 and 202-204: no minimum target sizes are applied. 'text-3xl/10 font-medium lg:text-sm/7' 'rounded-full p-1.5' 'size-6' ```
issue_description.expected_vs_actual: | Aspect | Expected | Actual | |--------|----------|--------| | **Desktop navigation** | Every nav target is at least 40px high | Link height follows the 28px text line box | | **Mobile navigation** | Every nav target is at least 44px high | Link height follows a 40px line box | | **Menu controls** | Open and close controls are 44px squares on mobile | 24px icon plus 6px padding produces a 36px square | | **Component parity** | Both navbar variants share the control-size contract | Only the active centered-logo variant has compliant sizing |

summary.title: [CTA Links] Action targets remain below minimum size
summary.one_line_description: Feature and 404 calls to action remain 28px high, missing the plan's 40px desktop and 44px mobile target contract.

bug_4.file_locations: - /Users/sacino/bulma-root/demo/src/components/elements/link.tsx - function: Link, Lines 12-31 - /Users/sacino/bulma-root/demo/src/app/page.tsx - homepage feature CTAs, Lines 269-293 - /Users/sacino/bulma-root/demo/src/app/404/page.tsx - Go back home CTA, Lines 15-23 - /Users/sacino/bulma-root/demo/src/app/not-found.tsx - Go back home CTA, Lines 16-24 - /Users/sacino/bulma-root/demo/test/better-ui-contracts.test.mjs - surface contract coverage, Lines 120-142

issue_description.description: The implementation increases shared button, navbar, pricing-tab, footer, and lender targets, but the styled Link primitive still has only text-sm/7 line height and no minimum height or vertical padding. The non-prose Link instances used as the two homepage feature-card CTAs and both branded 404 CTAs therefore render with a 28px-high interaction box at desktop and mobile sizes. These are standalone actions rather than inline prose links, so they violate REQ-1 and the verification checklist requiring visible buttons and actionable links to be at least 40px on desktop and 44px at 390x900. The new contract test checks shared buttons and lender buttons but does not cover these Link-based CTA paths, allowing the incomplete target-size pass to remain green. Add an explicit CTA-sized Link variant or migrate these standalone actions to a compliant control while keeping inline prose links compact, then add route-level target assertions for the affected instances.
issue_description.expected_vs_actual: | Aspect | Expected | Actual | |--------|----------|--------| | Desktop target | Standalone feature and 404 actions are at least 40px high | Link box is 28px high | | Mobile target | Standalone feature and 404 actions are at least 44px high | Link box remains 28px high | | Regression coverage | Tests cover every standalone action class affected by REQ-1 | Structural tests cover buttons and lenders but omit Link-based CTAs |

summary.title: [Pricing] Dark selectors break module parity
summary.one_line_description: The homepage and pricing page use materially different selected-tab contrast in dark mode despite the required visual parity.

bug_5.file_locations: - /Users/sacino/bulma-root/demo/src/components/sections/pricing-multi-tier.tsx - function: PricingOptionToggle, Lines 62-100 - /Users/sacino/bulma-root/demo/src/components/sections/pricing-hero-multi-tier.tsx - function: ElasticTabToggle, Lines 80-163 - /Users/sacino/bulma-root/demo/src/app/page.tsx - homepage PricingMultiTier integration, Lines 461-574 - /Users/sacino/bulma-root/demo/src/app/pricing/page.tsx - pricing PricingHeroMultiTier integration, Lines 85-303 - /Users/sacino/bulma-root/demo/test/better-ui-contracts.test.mjs - pricing parity assertions, Lines 120-142

issue_description.description: The two pricing routes expose the same Monthly and Yearly choice but render incompatible selected states in dark mode. PricingOptionToggle on the homepage gives the selected button a solid white background with dark text, while ElasticTabToggle on /pricing uses a white 10% pill and keeps selected text white. The /pricing container itself is white 5%, so its selected pill differs from the track by only five percentage points and is much less legible than the homepage state. This violates the project pricing-module parity rule and the plan's instruction to keep both implementations visually aligned in every colour scheme and interaction state. The current contract test verifies the annual copy and source structure but never compares selected-tab colour classes. Consolidate the selector into a shared component or shared visual tokens so both routes use the same dark selected background, foreground, hover, and focus treatment, then assert computed parity in the required browser matrix.
issue_description.expected_vs_actual: | Aspect | Expected | Actual | |--------|----------|--------| | Dark selected state | Homepage and /pricing use the same selected-tab treatment | Homepage is solid white with dark text; /pricing is translucent white with white text | | Selection affordance | Selected option is immediately distinguishable in both modules | /pricing selected pill is white/10 over a white/5 track | | Regression coverage | Parity checks compare computed selected, hover, and focus styles | Test checks copy presence but not selector styling parity |

summary.title: [Pricing Toggle] Stale timers interrupt rapid reversals
summary.one_line_description: Each pricing-page tab click leaves an uncancelled timeout that can end a newer pill animation early.

bug_6.file_locations: - /Users/sacino/bulma-root/demo/src/components/sections/pricing-hero-multi-tier.tsx - function: ElasticTabToggle and handleSelect, Lines 80-115 - /Users/sacino/bulma-root/demo/src/app/pricing/page.tsx - PricingHeroMultiTier integration, Lines 85-303 - /Users/sacino/bulma-root/demo/test/better-ui-contracts.test.mjs - pricing interaction coverage, Lines 120-142

issue_description.description: ElasticTabToggle starts a new 500ms timeout on every selection and never stores or cancels the previous timeout. If a visitor selects Yearly and then returns to Monthly before the first 500ms window ends, the first timeout sets isAnimating to false while the second pill transition is still running. That state change replaces the active 400ms spring transition with the resting 300ms ease-out transition mid-flight, so the reversal can change speed or settle abruptly instead of remaining interruptible. The timer also survives component unmount, contrary to the project's requirement to clean up every timer created by animation code. Store the timeout handle, clear it before starting a replacement and during effect cleanup, and add a rapid Monthly-Yearly-Monthly interaction test that asserts the latest animation owns the full lifecycle.
issue_description.expected_vs_actual: | Aspect | Expected | Actual | |--------|----------|--------| | Rapid reversal | Latest selection retains its complete spring timing | Earlier timeout can switch the newer transition to resting timing mid-flight | | Timer ownership | At most one reset timer exists and unmount clears it | Every selection creates an independent timeout with no cleanup | | Regression coverage | Rapid tab reversal and unmount cleanup are exercised | Current pricing contract test is source-only and does not exercise timing |
